old_claim.note: Mass Production provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct edge remains from mass_production to interchangeable_parts because the source frames mass production as downstream of machine-tool-enabled interchangeable manufacture, not as its prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica says mass production came about through development of the machine-tool industry and precision equipment that produced large numbers of identical parts.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source places mass production after the machine-tool and interchangeable-parts capability, not before it.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Keeping mass_production as a prerequisite inverted the relationship and made a scaling outcome appear to be the causal substrate for interchangeable parts.

ontology_before: Interchangeable Parts depended on Mass Production, implying large-scale production was needed before interchangeable components.
ontology_after: Interchangeable Parts depends on precision machine tools; mass production remains a broader manufacturing outcome and downstream context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: the backwards mass_production prerequisite was removed.
would_reject_if: A source showed a specifically scoped mass-production method was required before industrial interchangeable parts could be produced.
The mass_production node were narrowed to pre-existing batch production machinery that directly produced interchangeable parts.
The interchangeable_parts node were rescoped from the manufacturing method to a later mass-production deployment practice.
short_reason: Mass production is downstream context here, not the prerequisite.
